7 min read
6 hours ago

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is a library in your node_modules that patches your browser.
Every setTimeout. Every Promise. Every click.
It intercepts all of them, silently, in every Angular app you have ever shipped.
Angular has depended on it since 2016. Ten years.
And now the Angular team is deleting it from new projects by default.
The library is zone.js. If you have never heard of it, that is exactly the problem, because it has been running underneath every line of Angular you have ever written. Today I am going to show you what it did, why it is going away, what replaces it, and the exact numbers I measured when I removed it from a real app.
Stay with me, because this is the biggest change to how Angular works since Angular 2 itself.
The Invisible Machine You Have Been Shipping
Here is what zone.js actually does, and most Angular developers go years without knowing this.
When your app boots, zone.js monkey-patches the browser’s async APIs. It wraps setTimeout, Promise, addEventListener, XHR, all of them, with its own versions. Why? So it can tap Angular on the shoulder every time…
